_meta.source: Repair manuals (Pelican Parts, AlldataDIY), YouTube pull videos, eBay listing photos, forum DIY guides
_meta.confidence: High for engine bay, Medium for interior modules, Verify for trim-specific parts
_meta.disclaimer: Locations vary by year/trim. Always verify visually before pulling.

instrument cluster.location: Behind dashboard, center of steering column
instrument cluster.access: Remove trim bezel (snap clips), 2-4 screws, disconnect harness. Some require steering wheel drop.
instrument cluster.notes: Watch for hidden screws behind dash trim. Label connectors with tape before disconnecting. Premium clusters (Denali, S-Line, M-Sport) have higher margins.
removal_steps: 1. Adjust steering wheel all the way down
2. Remove plastic trim bezel (pull straight out, clips only)
4. Pull cluster forward carefully — harness is short
instrument cluster.test_at_yard: Turn key to ON — gauges should sweep. Check for backlighting. LCD should show mileage (not dashes).
instrument cluster.factory_upgrade_potential: High — Base models often accept premium cluster swaps with coding

ECU.location: Engine bay passenger side firewall OR behind glove box OR under passenger kick panel
ECU.access: Remove plastic cover (clips) OR remove glove box (2 screws) OR remove kick panel
ECU.notes: Location varies significantly by make: Honda/Acura = passenger kick panel; Toyota/Lexus = engine bay firewall; GM = engine bay; BMW = under hood on passenger side; VW/Audi = behind glove box or under hood. Check eBay photos for your specific year to confirm.
removal_steps: 1. Locate ECU (check multiple locations if unsure)
3. Disconnect large harness connector(s) — squeeze lock tab
4. Note part number on label for compatibility checking
ECU.test_at_yard: Visual inspection only — cannot test without VIN-matched vehicle. Look for corrosion on connector pins. Check part number matches target vehicle.
ECU.common_failures: Water damage (engine bay location), corroded pins, fried from jump-start

ABS module.location: Engine bay, mounted to master cylinder/brake booster assembly
ABS module.access: Remove cover, disconnect brake lines (do NOT mix up! Mark with tape)
ABS module.notes: CRITICAL: Brake fluid will leak. Bring rags. Do NOT cross-thread lines on removal — if line is seized, skip it. Label lines with tape BEFORE disconnecting. The module is the electronic box ON TOP of the valve body, not the whole assembly.
removal_steps: 1. Remove plastic cover from master cylinder area
2. Label each brake line with tape (RF, LF, RR, LR)
3. Disconnect brake lines with flare nut wrench — expect fluid
5. Remove 4 mounting bolts — module lifts off valve body
ABS module.test_at_yard: Cannot fully test. Check for brake fluid leakage around module = bad seal. Look up common failures for this model year online.
ABS module.common_failures: Pump motor failure, solenoid codes (C-code), communication errors

throttle body.location: Intake manifold, top/front of engine — follow big intake hose from air filter
throttle body.access: Remove intake hose clamp, 4 bolts, disconnect harness and coolant hoses (if equipped)
throttle body.notes: Very accessible on most cars. Often has coolant lines attached (pinch clamps). Mark hose orientation if present. Some have 8mm bolts, not 10mm. Hybrid TBs (Prius) are electronically controlled and worth more.
removal_steps: 4. Remove coolant hoses if present (pinch clamps)
throttle body.test_at_yard: Visual — shaft should move freely by hand (engine off). Check for carbon buildup. Electronics side should be clean, not oil-soaked.
throttle body.common_failures: Carbon buildup causing idle issues, TPS sensor failure, motor failure on electronic TBs

amplifier.location: Trunk/rear cargo area OR under passenger seat OR behind glove box OR under rear deck
amplifier.access: Remove trunk panel clips OR lift seat forward OR remove glove box
amplifier.notes: Bose: often in trunk or rear deck. Harman Kardon: under seat or trunk. Mark Levinson: trunk or rear deck. Burmester/Bowers: trunk. Premium amps have heat sinks = easy ID. Check part number — some cars have multiple amp locations.
removal_steps: 1. Check trunk first — remove side panel clips (pull straight)
2. Look for black box with heat sinks and multiple connectors
3. If not in trunk, check under passenger seat (slide forward)
amplifier.test_at_yard: Visual only — look for water damage (trunk leaks = dead amp). Heat sinks should be clean, not corroded.
amplifier.factory_upgrade_potential: High — Many base cars have wiring for premium audio but no amp. Plug-and-play upgrade for owners.

BCM.location: Under driver dash OR behind glove box OR left of steering column OR under passenger kick panel
BCM.access: Remove lower dash panel (2-3 screws), locate module with multiple harnesses
BCM.notes: Usually black box with 3-5 large connectors. PHOTO connectors before removal — must go back same way. BCMs are VIN-locked on many newer cars (2010+) and require programming. Check interchange before buying. Some BCMs are integrated with fuse box.
removal_steps: 1. Remove lower dash panel (Phillips or 7mm screws)
2. Locate large black box with multiple connectors (3-5)
3. TAKE PHOTO of connector layout before touching
BCM.test_at_yard: Visual only. Check for water damage (leaky windshield = corroded BCM). Connector pins should be clean, not green.
BCM.common_failures: Water intrusion, corrupted software, failed internal relays
BCM.warning: BCMs often require dealer programming or VIN cloning. Verify interchangeability before pulling.

TCM.location: Passenger footwell OR engine bay on transmission OR under center console OR integrated into ECU
TCM.access: Remove kick panel OR transmission cover OR console trim
TCM.notes: Often integrated with ECU on newer cars. Check eBay listing photos to confirm separate module exists. Integrated TCM+ECU = harder pull but higher value. Standalone TCM = easier pull, moderate value. BMW ZF transmissions often have separate TCM on transmission itself.
removal_steps: 1. Check passenger footwell — remove kick panel
2. If not there, check transmission (remove plastic cover)
3. Some cars: under center console (remove trim)
TCM.test_at_yard: Visual only. Check part number against eBay listings for compatibility.
TCM.warning: TCMs often require programming for specific transmission. Verify interchange before pulling.

HID ballast.location: Under headlight assembly OR behind bumper cover OR attached to headlight housing OR near frame rail
HID ballast.access: Open hood, look for rectangular box ~3x5 inches attached to headlight wiring
HID ballast.notes: Small black box ~3x5 inches. Often attached to headlight housing or frame rail. Don't confuse with headlight leveling module (smaller). OEM ballasts (Osram, Philips, Denso, Matsushita) worth more than aftermarket. Both sides usually have separate ballasts.
removal_steps: 2. Look for black rectangular box with cooling fins
HID ballast.test_at_yard: Cannot test. Check for physical damage and corrosion. OEM labels (Osram, Philips) = higher value.
HID ballast.common_failures: Capacitor failure (common on 8-12 year old units), water intrusion

climate control.location: Center dash behind radio OR integrated with radio bezel
climate control.access: Remove radio trim (snap clips), 4 screws, disconnect harness
climate control.notes: Often includes blend door actuators. Pull the whole panel as assembly. Dual-zone climate = higher value. Rear climate controls (SUVs/minivans) = separate unit in back, also valuable.
removal_steps: 1. Remove center dash trim (pull straight, clips only)
3. Pull assembly forward — harness is short
5. Remove HVAC ducts if attached (squeeze clips)
climate control.test_at_yard: Turn key to ON — buttons should illuminate. LCD should display. Knobs should rotate smoothly.
climate control.factory_upgrade_potential: Medium — Some base cars accept dual-zone upgrade with wiring harness swap

fuel pump.location: Top of fuel tank, under rear seat OR trunk floor panel
fuel pump.access: Lift rear seat (clips) OR remove trunk panel, disconnect fuel lines
tools: Fuel line disconnect tool ($5 at parts store)
fuel pump.notes: Tank may be 1/4 full = heavy. Fuel vapors = NO SPARKS/NO SMOKING. Ring retainer may be rusted — bring PB Blaster. Some cars have access panel under seat (easy), others require tank drop (skip unless very valuable).
removal_steps: 1. Lift rear seat or remove trunk floor panel
3. Clean area around ring — debris falls in tank
4. Remove retainer ring (hammer + chisel if rusted)
5. Disconnect fuel lines with disconnect tool
fuel pump.test_at_yard: Cannot test. Visual — check for corrosion on connector. Screen/filter should be intact.
fuel pump.safety_warning: NO SMOKING. Fuel vapors are explosive. Work in well-ventilated area.

turbocharger.location: Exhaust manifold side, center of engine bay — look for snail-shaped housing
turbocharger.access: Remove heat shield, intake tubing, exhaust flange bolts, oil/coolant lines
turbocharger.notes: Rusty exhaust bolts = PB Blaster first. Let sit 10 min. Heavy part — support before final bolt removal. Oil/coolant lines attached — expect drips. Don't lose copper washers on banjo bolts. Check shaft play — grab compressor wheel, should have minimal play.
removal_steps: 1. Soak exhaust flange nuts with PB Blaster (wait 10 min)
7. Remove mounting bolts — support turbo before last bolt
turbocharger.test_at_yard: Grab compressor wheel (intake side) — should spin freely with minimal shaft play. Oil in compressor = bad seals. Check for cracked housing.

seat control.location: Under driver seat OR integrated into seat base OR under seat track
seat control.access: Slide seat all the way back, look underneath for small black box
seat control.notes: Memory seat cars always have module. Base models may not. Check for heated seat buttons as indicator. Module is small black box with 2-3 connectors. Some integrate into seat motor assembly.
removal_steps: 1. Slide seat all the way back (or forward for access)
2. Look under seat for black box with connectors
seat control.test_at_yard: Visual only. Check connector for corrosion.
seat control.factory_upgrade_potential: High — Many cars have wiring for memory/heat but no module. Plug-and-play upgrade.

alternator.location: Front of engine, driven by serpentine belt — look for round device with cooling vents
alternator.access: Remove belt (tensioner), 2-3 bolts, disconnect harness and battery cable
tools: Serpentine belt tool or 3/8 drive ratchet
alternator.notes: Very accessible on most cars. Note belt routing before removal (take photo). The rear bolt is often hard to reach — use extension + swivel. High-output alternators (trucks, police cars, ambulances) = premium.
alternator.test_at_yard: Spin pulley by hand — should spin smoothly, no grinding. Check for burned smell. Rear bearing should not wobble.
alternator.common_failures: Worn bearings (grinding), voltage regulator failure, diode failure

MAF sensor.location: Intake duct between air filter and throttle body — rectangular plastic housing
MAF sensor.access: Unclip sensor from intake tube, disconnect harness
tools: MAF cleaner spray (optional for cleaning later)
MAF sensor.notes: Easiest pull on the list. Don't touch sensor wires with fingers — oils damage them. Some use hose clamp, others use 2 screws (Torx). OEM Bosch/Denso sensors worth more than aftermarket.
removal_steps: 1. Locate sensor housing in intake tube
3. Loosen hose clamp or remove 2 Torx screws
4. Pull sensor straight out — DO NOT touch internal wires
MAF sensor.test_at_yard: Visual — wires should be clean, not coated with dirt/oil. Housing should not be cracked.
MAF sensor.common_failures: Contaminated wires (from oiled air filter), cracked housing, connector corrosion

radio nav.location: Center dash, behind bezel — look for rectangular opening with screen
radio nav.access: Remove dash trim (clips), 4 mounting screws, disconnect harness/antenna
radio nav.notes: Premium nav units often have separate DVD drive below. Pull both. GPS antenna on dash top. Amplifier may be separate (trunk/under seat). OEM nav units with Apple CarPlay/Android Auto = GOLD. Aftermarket = skip.
removal_steps: 1. Remove center dash trim (pull straight, clips)
3. Pull radio forward — harness is short
7. Check for separate DVD drive below — remove too
radio nav.test_at_yard: Turn key to ACC — screen should illuminate. Touchscreen should respond. CD/DVD should accept disc.
radio nav.factory_upgrade_potential: Very High — Base cars accept premium nav with coding. CarPlay/Android Auto units = $500-1000 demand.

catalytic converter.location: Under vehicle, in exhaust system between engine and muffler — look for bulging canister
catalytic converter.access: Jack/stands or ramp required. Usually accessible from underneath.
catalytic converter.notes: THE most valuable part on most vehicles. Toyota Prius cats especially valuable ($1,000+). Foreign/luxury cats worth 2-5x domestic. Check converter type: ceramic honeycomb (light, fragile) vs metallic (heavy, dense). Some have heat shields that must be removed first.
removal_steps: 1. Locate converter — follow exhaust from engine, look for bulge
2. Remove heat shield if present (10mm bolts)
3. Soak flange nuts with PB Blaster (wait 10 min)
6. If bolts are seized, use Sawzall to cut pipe
catalytic converter.test_at_yard: Cannot test at yard. Visual — look for intact honeycomb (shine flashlight through). OEM stamp = higher value. Aftermarket cats have minimal value.
catalytic converter.value_factors: Vehicle make (Toyota/Honda/Ford trucks = high), converter type (foreign = premium), precious metal prices (Rhodium up 73% in 2025)
catalytic converter.warning: Some yards remove cats before cars enter lot. Check before targeting specific vehicle.

A/C compressor.location: Front of engine, driven by serpentine belt — cylindrical unit with clutch plate
A/C compressor.access: Remove belt, disconnect refrigerant lines, remove 4 mounting bolts
tools: Note: EPA requires refrigerant recovery — but at junkyard, lines are already empty
A/C compressor.notes: At junkyard, refrigerant is usually already escaped. If lines are intact, assume pressure escaped. Clutch plate should spin freely by hand. Hybrid A/C compressors (electric, not belt-driven) = much higher value.
A/C compressor.test_at_yard: Spin clutch plate by hand — should rotate smoothly. Check for seized clutch (won't spin).
A/C compressor.common_failures: Seized clutch, internal failure (won't compress), leaking shaft seal

power steering pump.location: Front of engine, driven by serpentine belt — small pump with fluid reservoir
power steering pump.access: Remove belt, disconnect pressure lines, remove 3 mounting bolts
power steering pump.notes: Electric power steering (2010+) = module on rack or column, no pump. These modules are valuable. Hydraulic pumps = moderate value. Look for rack-mounted motor on newer cars.
removal_steps: 2. Disconnect pressure and return lines (catch fluid)
power steering pump.test_at_yard: Spin pulley by hand — should turn smoothly. Check for leaks around shaft seal.
power steering pump.note_electric: Electric steering modules: located on rack or steering column. Look for module with cooling fins and multiple connectors.

wheels.access: Remove lug nuts (17mm, 19mm, or 21mm socket), wheel lifts off
wheels.notes: Alloy wheels worth more than steel. Check for curb rash, cracks, bends. OEM wheels from performance models (M-Sport, AMG, S-Line) command premium. Full sets worth more than singles. Matching center caps = bonus.
wheels.test_at_yard: Visual — curb rash reduces value. Check for cracks in spokes. Spin wheel on ground — should roll true (not wobble).

ABS DSC module.location: Engine bay, mounted to brake master cylinder / ABS pump assembly
ABS DSC module.access: Remove plastic cover, disconnect brake lines (mark with tape), unbolt module from pump
ABS DSC module.notes: BMW-specific naming. Same physical location as ABS module but may include DSC controller. Look for module with DSC label.
removal_steps: 1. Locate ABS/DSC module on master cylinder assembly
ABS DSC module.test_at_yard: Visual inspection for corrosion. Check part number label. Cannot test without matched vehicle.

BOSE amplifier.location: Trunk/hatch area, under cargo floor or behind interior panel
BOSE amplifier.access: Remove cargo trim panel or lift floor panel (snap clips)
BOSE amplifier.notes: Often mounted to trunk side panel or under spare tire well. Look for BOSE-branded silver box with cooling fins. Verify part number before pulling.
removal_steps: 2. Locate BOSE amp (silver box with fins)
BOSE amplifier.test_at_yard: Visual only — check for water damage if in trunk well. Corrosion = skip.
BOSE amplifier.factory_upgrade_potential: Medium — Bose amps fit multiple GM/Cadillac models

ECU DME.location: Engine bay passenger side firewall OR under hood on passenger side
ECU DME.notes: BMW-specific term (Digital Motor Electronics). Same location as ECU. Often mounted in a black plastic box with cooling fins. Part number on label is critical for matching.
removal_steps: 1. Locate DME box on passenger side firewall
ECU DME.test_at_yard: Visual inspection only. Check for corrosion on connector pins. Match part number exactly.

ECU PCM.location: Engine bay firewall OR behind glove box OR under passenger kick panel
ECU PCM.access: Remove plastic cover OR remove glove box (2 screws) OR remove kick panel
ECU PCM.notes: GM/Chrysler/Dodge term (Powertrain Control Module). Same as ECU. Location varies: GM = engine bay; Chrysler = behind glove box or under hood. Check eBay photos for your specific year.
ECU PCM.test_at_yard: Visual inspection only. Check for corrosion. Match part number.

MMI amp.location: Trunk/hatch area, under cargo floor or behind left trunk panel
MMI amp.notes: Audi-specific (Multi Media Interface). Usually mounted in trunk left side or under spare tire well. Look for silver/black amp box with Audi part number.
removal_steps: 1. Remove trunk left side trim panel
MMI amp.test_at_yard: Visual only. Check for water damage.

Mark Levinson amp.location: Trunk/hatch area, under cargo floor or behind right trunk panel
Mark Levinson amp.notes: Lexus/Toyota premium audio. Often mounted under cargo floor or behind right trunk panel. Look for Mark Levinson branded amp or part numbers starting with 86280.
removal_steps: 1. Remove trunk right side trim panel
Mark Levinson amp.test_at_yard: Visual only. Premium amps command higher prices.

SRS airbag module.location: Under center console OR under passenger seat OR behind glove box
SRS airbag module.access: Remove console trim (snap clips) OR slide seat fully back OR remove glove box
SRS airbag module.notes: Airbag control module. Location varies: Honda = under center console; Toyota = under passenger seat; GM = behind glove box. Often yellow connector. Handle with care.
removal_steps: 1. Locate module (check console, seat, glove box)
SRS airbag module.test_at_yard: Visual only. Check part number. Some modules store crash data and need resetting.

body control module.location: Under driver dash, left of steering column OR behind glove box
body control module.access: Remove lower dash panel (snap clips + screws) OR remove glove box
body control module.notes: Same as BCM. Controls windows, locks, lights, wipers. Often mounted to fuse panel area. May be integrated with fuse box.
removal_steps: 2. Locate BCM (often black box with multiple connectors)
5. Disconnect harness connectors one at a time
body control module.test_at_yard: Visual only. Check for corrosion. Match part number.

headlight assembly.access: Open hood, remove bolts from top of assembly, pull forward
headlight assembly.notes: Halogen units are cheap. HID/Xenon and LED assemblies are high-value. Check for adaptive/auto-leveling features. Look for damage to lens or tabs.
removal_steps: 5. Check for hidden lower bolts on some models
headlight assembly.test_at_yard: Check lens for cracks. Verify mounting tabs intact. HID ballasts may be separate part.

ignition coil.location: On top of engine, one per cylinder (coil-on-plug)
ignition coil.access: Remove engine cover (snap clips), pull coil straight up
ignition coil.notes: Individual coils are low margin. Complete set of 4-8 coils is better. Check for oil contamination (indicates valve cover leak).
removal_steps: 4. Pull coil straight up — do NOT twist
ignition coil.test_at_yard: Check for cracks in coil body. Oil in boot = skip. Verify part number matches.

navigation radio.access: Remove dash trim (snap clips), remove 4 screws, slide unit out
navigation radio.notes: Same as radio/navigation. Premium units with NAV and touchscreen are high-value. Check for anti-theft codes. Part number usually on side or back of unit.
navigation radio.test_at_yard: Visual only. Check screen for cracks. Premium NAV units worth more.

oxygen sensor.location: Exhaust manifold/pipe, before and after catalytic converter
oxygen sensor.access: From under vehicle or hood. May need socket with slot for wire.
oxygen sensor.notes: Upstream (pre-cat) sensors are more valuable. OEM Bosch, Denso, NTK command premium. Aftermarket generics are low margin.
removal_steps: 3. Use O2 sensor socket to remove
4. Cut wire if needed (yard doesn't care)
oxygen sensor.test_at_yard: Check branding. OEM sensors worth 3-5x aftermarket. Look for part number stamping.

starter.location: Lower engine block, where transmission meets engine
starter.access: From under vehicle. Remove lower splash shield if present.
starter.notes: Heavy and awkward. May require removing exhaust crossover pipe on some V6/V8 engines. Check for solenoid damage.
removal_steps: 1. Jack up vehicle and secure on stands
4. Disconnect battery cable and solenoid wire
starter.test_at_yard: Check gear teeth for wear. Spin test if battery available. Heavy = higher shipping cost.

taillight assembly.location: Rear of vehicle, inside trunk/hatch area
taillight assembly.access: Open trunk, remove interior trim panel (snap clips), remove nuts/bolts
taillight assembly.notes: LED and smoked/tinted assemblies command premium. Check for cracks in lens. Verify all mounting tabs intact.
removal_steps: 2. Remove interior trim panel behind light
taillight assembly.test_at_yard: Check lens for cracks. Verify tabs. LED units worth more than incandescent.

window switch.location: Driver door panel OR center console
window switch.access: Remove door panel (snap clips + screws behind trim) OR pry up console switch
window switch.notes: Master switch (driver side, all windows) is highest value. Individual door switches are lower margin. Check for broken tabs on connector.
removal_steps: 1. Remove door panel or pry switch from console
window switch.test_at_yard: Visual only. Check for cracked plastic. Master switch = higher value.
